entry.title: Customer & Partner Roundtable for SAP BTP ABAP Environment #23 - demo time in ADT
entry.content:
👉 The call for contributions for one of the upcoming roundtables is open! 

 

If you want to show a demo or share a use case scenario for SAP BTP ABAP Environment send us an email and we will get back to you.
 

Introduction

 

As SAP BTP ABAP environment (aka Steampunk) and ABAP Cloud became more popular inside and outside of SAP, there is a high demand for rolling out the latest product news and updates, asking questions, and showing demos. 

💡 You can find the slides presented, recordings, and further references from the previous roundtables in this GitHub repository.


Meeting Information

When:                                                                      
  • May 20th, 10:00 - 11:00 AM CET  – Zoom Meeting (please register in advance) 
 
Who:
  • All interested customers, partners, and stakeholders are invited to join this roundtable
  • BTP ABAP team:
    • Frank Jentsch (Product Lead for SAP BTP ABAP Environment)
    • Iwona Hahn (Product Management)
    • Nora Klemp (Product Management)
    • Shyam Balachandran (Development Expert)
    • Christian Cop (Development Expert)
    • Dr. Oliver Brein (Development Expert)
    • Simon Dold (Developer)
Preliminary Agenda:
  • Product update for SAP BTP ABAP Environment release 2505
  • Demos in ABAP Development Tools (ADT):
    • Quick Fiori app generation (Shyam Balachandran)
    • Swagger support for OData services (Shyam Balachandran)
    • UX improvements of generator framework (Shyam Balachandran)
    • Getting SQL Insights with HANA Plan Trace integrated in ADT Cross Trace (Christian Cop & Dr. Oliver Brein)
    • Enhancements of Manage Software Components app (Simon Dold)
  • Q&A 
Looking forward to meeting you!
 


Check out our SAP Business Technology ABAP Environment page in SAP Community for product updates and upcoming events.

entry.title: SAP Boosts ABAP Code Generation with Constrained Decoding and NVIDIA NIM
entry.content:
Generative AI has transformed how developers work by streamlining coding tasks and accelerating the transition from concepts to functional code. ABAP is SAP’s proprietary programming language and a critical foundation for SAP’s ERP systems and SAP S/4HANA Cloud, making it a crucial target for specialized AI.

Earlier this year, we unveiled the general availability of Joule for Developers in ABAP Development Tools (ADT). To bring this to life, we built an ABAP large language model (LLM) trained on more than 300 million lines of internal ABAP code as well as natural language documentation. A key capability of Joule for Developers is predictive code completion which suggests code snippets in real time in a “context-aware” fashion.

In this blog post we introduce how we leverage Constrained Decoding for code generation to 1) accelerate development time, 2) reduce errors, and 3) increase compliance and reliability. Throughout this process, we collaborated closely with NVIDIA to leverage NVIDIA NIM ™ microservices for efficient inference.

 

  Figure 1: Joule for Developers integrated in ABAP Development Tools (ADT)

 

The importance of generating reliable and compliant ABAP syntax

Because ABAP is a closed-source and low-resource language, general purpose commercial LLMs often struggle to produce correct syntax and follow ABAP cloud programming best practices. Their performance is subpar compared to their capabilities in more commonly used languages such as python and Java. To close the performance gap of available LLMs on ABAP, we trained our own ABAP foundational model using internal ABAP code as well as high quality, manually labeled data. Given the complexity of the business logic involved, even minor syntax or logical errors can lead to costly disruptions.  Adhering to SAP-specific standards is therefore crucial.

Introducing constrained code generation using an ultra-fast ABAP parser

Constrained decoding revolves around verifying each token or entire statements in real time against ABAP’s strict grammar rules and SAP’s evolving standards. If a partial suggestion seems wrong, whether due to syntax, language-version differences (such as ABAP Cloud), or SAP-specific compliance rules, the parser instantly flags and refines it, ensuring that only correct suggestions are presented to the user.

Figure 2 shows how constrained decoding works using a simple example. Every potential token sequence (e.g., “value1 + value2”) is evaluated against the ABAP grammar. If the most likely next token or statement would violate ABAP syntax (such as prematurely ending a class, or creating an unbalanced block boundary), the parser flags it as invalid and discards that path. This pruning process guarantees that the final output strictly adheres to the defined ABAP rules and structure.

In detail, if {x₁, x₂, …, xᵢ₋₁} is the partial sequence generated so far. At step i, we let the language model define a probability distribution over potential next tokens t as:

P_model (t | x₁, …, xᵢ₋₁).

We denote by G(x₁, …, xᵢ₋₁, t) ∈ {True, False} a grammar-validation function powered by the ABAP parser that evaluates whether the sequence (x₁, …, xᵢ₋₁, t) adheres to ABAP syntax. The set of valid next tokens is then:

Vᵢ = { t | G(x₁, …, xᵢ₋₁, t) = True }.

The constrained decoding step then selects the highest probability token from the valid set. By ensuring that t must lie in Vᵢ, the process discards any token violating ABAP syntax, thereby boosting the grammar-compliance of the sequence. The same procedure can be applied to restrict the generations to special ABAP language versions, such as ABAP cloud-compliant objects and APIs.

 

 Figure 2: Token-wise Constrained Decoding for ABAP

 

This approach hinges on the speed and accuracy of the ABAP parser to evaluate each potential next token during inference. To ensure it works effectively we implemented a Rust-based parser and collaborated with NVIDIA to integrate it into the inference engine in NVIDIA NIM.

Evaluating Constrained Generation for ABAP

To quantify how well constrained decoding contributes to generating working ABAP code, we assembled a diverse benchmark of real-world ABAP Cloud programming tasks. Each programming task is accompanied with several unit tests that check correct and cloud compliant implementation. So, the simplest, most objective metric becomes: does the generated code pass all its unit tests? This is referred to as the Unit Test Pass Rate (Figure 3).

 

 Figure 3: Performance benchmark for a diverse set of ABAP code generation tasks

 

GPT-4, a standard state-of-the-art LLM, passes just over half of our ABAP Cloud tests, underscoring how niche language constructs and SAP-specific APIs can trip up generic models. Simply switching to our internally trained ABAP model lifts the pass rate to 76%. This model has seen ABAP grammar, definitions, and APIs that are relevant for day-to-day development. Through token-wise and statement-wise constrained decoding and sampling we further boosted the performance to 84% enabling additional tasks to be solved automatically.

 

Collaborated with NVIDIA to Add Support for SAP ABAP Constrained Decoding to NIM

Deploying constrained decoding for ABAP code generation hinges on having a flexible, high-performance LLM inference framework – so we’ve collaborated with NVIDIA to leverage NIM. NVIDIA NIM is a set of easy-to-use microservices designed to accelerate the deployment of generative AI models across the cloud, data centers, and workstations. Together with NVIDIA, SAP designed and developed a custom guided decoding capability leveraging NIM, which provided the key features below for an effective implementation of ABAP constrained decoding in the LLM inference pipeline.

 

Seamless Integration

NIM's API allows for straightforward integration of our custom logits processor and ABAP parser. Figure 4 shows the architectural diagram of how ABAP constrained decoding is integrated and deployed via a NIM container.


 Figure 4: Architecture of ABAP Constrained Decoding in NIM

 

 

By implementing the logit processing functionality through a Python interface, we inject our grammar validation logic directly into the token generation process. This means that each potential token can be evaluated against ABAP grammar rules in real-time, effectively pruning invalid paths before they're suggested to the developer. Our custom implementations can be stored as packaged directories and loaded into NIM at runtime.

 

Flexible Runtime Selection

One significant advantage of NIM is its support for multiple custom decoding backends, with the ability to switch between them on the fly per user query. This flexibility allows us to employ different parsing strategies based on the specific ABAP context (such as switching between standard ABAP and ABAP Cloud constraints) without needing a separate deployment for each ABAP language version.

 

Optimized Performance

Despite the additional validation steps, the Rust-based ABAP parser integration through NIM maintains high throughput and low latency. The highly optimized infrastructure handles the token-by-token validation efficiently, ensuring that code suggestions appear promptly in the developer's environment. Our benchmarks show that the constrained decoding approach adds minimal overhead compared to unconstrained generation, while significantly improving the quality and compliance of the suggestions.

When compared with alternative baseline inference solution for a typical server request workload, our co-designed NIM achieves up to 31% better latency and up to 26% better throughput. Figure 5 shows various normalized latency and throughput metrics.


 Figure 5: Latency and Throughput Comparison of Co-Designed NIM Solution vs. Alternative

  

State Management

NIM's state management capabilities proved invaluable for complex ABAP syntax validation that requires context tracking (such as nested code blocks or statement boundaries). By leveraging the context variables feature, our parser can maintain awareness of the current syntax state throughout the token generation process, ensuring that suggestions remain contextually appropriate.

 

Summary

At SAP, we continually innovate to improve ABAP developer productivity. Constrained decoding is one such technique. This approach elevates Joule for Developers’ code completion functionality by narrowing the model’s output space with a millisecond‑fast Rust parser. With NVIDIA, we co-designed our solution to leverage a custom decoding backend to deliver constrained decoding with optimized performance powered by NIM.  Looking ahead, we will extend the grammar layer to accommodate additional constraints, updated APIs and evolving SAP guidelines.

 

 

Authored by: Abdelaziz Ben Othman, Salma Sohrabi-Jahromi, Karthikeyan Asokkuma, Philipp Herzig; and NVIDIA: Yi Fan, Neal Vaidya, Jeff Farris, Bhuvan Agrawal, Arun Venkatesan, Tricia Barr.


The following people (written in alphabetic order) contributed substantially to developing the implementation discussed in this post: Abdelaziz Ben Othman, Akhilesh Kakade, Ankit Shrivastava, Kai Patrick Reisert, and Sai Madhav Jonnada.

entry.title: Join our Live Influencing Session at ASUG Annual Conference in Orlando!
entry.content:
Customer Influence: Shaping the Future of AI-Driven Development with Joule

Be part of the conversation that's shaping the future of development in SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP!

Join SAP experts and your peers for an interactive Live Influencing Session focused on ABAP Cloud, CAP, and the game-changing role of Joule.

✅Gain early insights into AI-driven, cloud-ready development
✅Share your real-world experiences and challenges
✅Collaborate directly with SAP product teams
✅Influence future innovations through live feedback and discussion

Whether you're hands-on with ABAP Cloud, building on SAP BTP, or exploring how AI can accelerate development, this session is your chance to make your voice heard and shape what comes next.

Date: Wednesday, May 21
Time: 2:00–2:40 PM
Location: ASUG Hub – Theater 1 (on the show floor)

Add it to your ASUG Annual Conference agenda – and help influence SAP’s products!

entry.title: S/4 HANA 2023 | Enable Dynamic Feature Controls and Side Effects in RAPM based Fiori Apps
entry.content:
Background

Upon upgrading our SAP system from S/4 HANA 2021 to the 2023 version, we experienced difficulties with our RAP-based Fiori apps. Specifically, we found that Feature Controls and Side Effects were not functioning correctly. Despite adhering to the new syntax, issues with those Fiori applications continued. Ultimately, we collaborated with the SAP support team and learned that certain modifications were necessary to enable those features in the S/4 2023 version.

Given the absence of an SCN post or comprehensive documentation detailing the steps to activate Feature Controls and Side Effects in the new version, I've put together this post using insights gained from our experience. While this post might not encompass all scenarios or specifics related to Feature Controls and Side Effects in RAP, it does specifically address the solutions/concept to enable Dynamic Feature Controls for UI elements and Side Effects associated with fields and actions in the new version.

In the S/4 HANA 2023 version, changes have been made to the RAP framework to boost the performance of Fiori elements applications. Consequently, we need to make some modifications to enable the Dynamic Feature Control and Side Effects.

Enable Dynamic Feature Controls

Example Use Case: When Fiori application has a Business Partner drop down with Customer and Supplier, if the Customer is selected then the Customer Number field should be changed to a mandatory field automatically in the UI.

To achieve above requirement in S/4 2023 version, following steps needs to be implemented.

  1. With dynamic feature control, you can add access restrictions based on conditions or specify access restrictions for fields. The field needs to be annotated as shown below in the behavior definition and is implemented in the behavior pool (step 2).                                                                                                                            field ( features : instance ) kunnr;                                                                                            
  2. In the method get_instance_features of the Behavior Pool, we need to implement respective logic for setting field behavior. Below syntax sets the customer number field to mandatory field.                                                     <lfs_result>-%field-kunnr = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-mandatory.                                                      
  3. Read the Metadata file of the service from Gateway or from the Browser Developers Tool to find the name of the respective field control (in this case kunnr_fc) in the respective Entity Type section as shown below.                                                                                            <Property Name="kunnr_fc" Type="Edm.Byte" sap:label="Dyn. Field Control"                                                                               sap:creatable="false" sap:updatable="false" sap:sortable="false" sap:filterable="false"/>                                                                        
  4. Add the relevant field controls in the target properties (in this case kunnr_fc) for the field in the source properties, in the local Annotations.xml file in Gateway UI application as shown below. In this case, the sourcefieldname will be the field name for the business partner dropdown. Please note that the Qualifier must be unique for every Annotation tag.          Similar steps can be followed to enable Dynamic Feature Controls for Operations and Actions.

Enable Side Effects for Determinations

Example Use Case: When a Customer Number in the Fiori Application is changed, the Customer Name and Customer Country values of the respective Customer should be updated automatically in the UI.

  1. Add Use Side Effects statement in the Behavior Projection.                                                                                                      use side effects;                                                                                                                    
  2. In the Behavior Definition, we need to declare the source and target entities for the side effects as shown below.                                                                                                                                                                            side effects {  field kunnr affects field name1, field land1;  }                                                               
  3. Declare the Determination for the Side Effect entity as shown below. You can also specify action for which the below determination should be triggered (example, create only or update only)                                            determination updateCustomerAddress on modify { <action> ; field kunnr;  }                                      
  4. Implement the method generated for the above determination in the behavior implementation class, to determine customer name and customer country values and implement EML modify entity on BO instance to update the fields.

Related Information:

  1. In the S/4 2023 version, for the RAP applications, mentioning the strict mode (use strict;) in Behavior Definition Projection is required. When a BO is defined as strict, set of best-practice rules are technically enforced with additional syntax checks on specific design and implementation requirements to make sure that a BO is lifecycle-stable, upgrade-safe, and best-practice compliant.                                                                       
  2. In S/4 2023 version, there is no support for DDIC based CDS views, they need to be migrated to CDS view entities. Use migrate wizard in Eclipse to migrate your DDIC based CDS views to CDS entities.                                                                                                           
  3. Use UI5 inspector (chrome extension) to inspect the App UI5 controls for additional information in the runtime (example: Aggregations, Bindings,Properties etc.).                                                                             
  4. In Browser Developer Tools, Use Sources -> ApplicationController-dbg.js, to inspect runtime values for side effects.                                                                                                                                                              
  5. In S/4 2023 or higher versions, It is recommended to maintain all the possible side effects or annotations in the backend itself instead of moving them to the frontend (local annotations.xml), for the reusability, maintenance, and better support for future upgrades.

References:

  1. How to use side effects in RAP https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/how-to-use-side-effects-in-rap-restful-application-programming-model/ba-p/13576698
  2. RAP Side Effects Document https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-rap/side-effects
  3. Instance Feature Controls Document https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-rap/instance-feature-control
  4. Strict Mode https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-rap/strict-mode
  5. DDIC based CDS views to CDS entities migration https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-cds-tools-user-guide/migrating-cds-ddic-based-views-to-cds-view-entities

entry.title: Project Kernseife - improving the measurement of Clean Core in ABAP
entry.content:
TL;DR

To improve the handling and measuring of classic ABAP code in ATC with regards to clean core, the possibility of an ATC check based on a custom classification was developed and is now published as an open source project named "Project Kernseife".

 

Challenges in Measuring Clean Core

Working in the area of clean core - especially focusing on the dimension "extensibility" - in projects of different scales, we were more than once asked in the last years on how to measure and govern the clean core principles within SAP S/4HANA systems (focusing on Private Cloud & On-Premises).

A key part to describe the "clean core state" of extensions is from our point of view the creation of a technical debt score that should align with the governance process in your system. That is why SAP's own code quality tool ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC) is very helpful on this. Since the release of "ABAP Cloud" as target for On-Stack extensions, we have now possibibilities with the related ATC Check Variant (ABAP_CLOUD_READINESS) to identify the "distance to Tier 1".

But looking at landscapes with large amount of legacy code (e.g. > 100,000 custom code objects in classic ABAP) or systems where you cannot use sufficiently released APIs for Tier 1, it becomes more difficult to identify the "real" technical debt.

If you run with the default ABAP_CLOUD_READINESS variant into larger systems, you might be overwhelmed by a huge number of findings in ATC - that are all marked as Error. This is logical, if you want to identify the criticality as "what do I need to change to get into ABAP Cloud", but at the same time the important technical debt might be hidden behind a lot of "false positive" findings.

To improve this situation, SAP released the new 3 Tier check (SAP Note 3565942) in February of 2025. This check improves the situation by analyzing "classic" coding with a stronger focus on technical debt, to identify the "right" things. It is based on SAP's released objects (Tier 1, official APIs) and nominated Classic APIs. These Classic APIs belong either to business logic (e.g. BAPI_PO_CREATE1) or a technical framework (e.g. ALV). Finally, there are now also "noAPIs" (objects that must not be used, e.g. RFC_READ_TABLE) nominated by SAP.

Still - we saw a gap in projects to improve the lifes of lead developers: A custom classification. Because SAP may not be able to classify a certain ABAP object as precise as you may be able to do. So SAP is not publishing any information on a given ABAP object and it is shown as accessing an internal object (which leads in ATC to a warning). But in your use case, you are fine with using it. And this is where Kernseife can help you.

 

What is "Kernseife"?

Kernseife consists of two parts:

  1. A custom ATC Check that is based on the new Tier 3 check mentioned above, but uses a custom classification system.
  2. CAP Application to create a custom classification of ABAP objects (and we even use it to analyze results from systems in a simple way).

ATC Check for custom classification

As mentioned, we started to work on "Kernseife" in 2024 as there was no detailed Tier 3 check available then. Therefore, our focus was initially to create a custom ATC check that can work with a custom classification. It is now re-worked and based on the "new" ATC Check (Note 3565942).

This check is basically analyzing your custom code like the Tier 3 check, but when it comes to the evaluation of the impact of a finding, the custom classification comes into play. You can upload the custom classification as a JSON file and in this classification will then used to determine your findings. Let's explain the difference with an example:

In your project, you need to work with the RF framework, because you are implementing EWM in your Private Cloud Environment. Therefore, you need to work with the classes /SCWM/CL_RF*, that are the recommended way to interact with the RF framework. If you look into the Cloudifcation Repostory, you can see that these classes are not released or nominated as "classic API". Still, you want to generally accept the usage of the RF framework for both: Governance of developers (ATC Checks) and measuring your "distance to clean core".

By using your custom classification which contains the classes /SCWM/CL_RF* as "Business Function - Recommended" (BF1), which will lead to only "Information" (Prio 3) findings in the check and will have a lower impact on the "clean core score", you can improve your efficiency.

For details on the custom classification, you can check the classification guide.

Application to manage Classifications

Of course, you could maintain the JSON file of the classification manually based on the given json format. But we created a little app, that is currently in a feature branch, to do that in a more sophisticated way.

In this application, you can initially check the current state for objects as defined by SAP and additionally add objects that are not yet mentioned by SAP.

Sticking with the example from above, you can now edit the "Rating Code" of the /SCWM/CL_RF* classes to "BF1". After you did your changes, you can download the classification JSON file, upload it into your ATC check system and all of the findings for these classes will in future be recorded as "Information" (Prio 3) message.

 

Governance and Measurability

With this custom classification and the usage in ABAP Test Cockpit, we can now combine the governance view and the measurability within ATC.

Governance

By enhancing SAP's default rating, you can control your developers more efficiently. Because as of today, your lead developers that are able to approve exemptions might be overwhelmed by a lot of "false positive" ATC findings. Just imagine every usage of the example classes "/SCWM/CL_RF*" need to be exempted.

In projects - especially looking at the overwhelming errors from the "ABAP_CLOUD_READINESS" ATC check in classic ABAP, you can now control with your custom classification, that recurring "false positive" findings will disappear (or become prio 3 messages only).

This is important, as - being a reviewer - your chances to ask questions on the important findings will improve. Because if for every major developments you need to approve hundreds of exemption requests, you will most probably go into "autopilot" approval mode as you cannot check all of the requests in detail.

Measurability

Our second motivation to create this check is to create a more realistic impact rating to measure what we call the "Kernseife Score" (basically: a technical debt score per object). With every classification (BF1), we consider a score of how severe the impact of using this SAP object is with regards to clean core.

So you can configure now a score - we did use a 0 to 10 scale - to identify how severe is a single finding. Based on the ATC check, we can now provide per object a score that you can use for your reporting. Either to identify the total score per system, per package, per object - or even to deep dive into analyses like "which object has the worst average score per finding?" or "which object is in total view the "uncleanest" object in my system?".

While you can then easily use your analytics tools (e.g. SAP Analytics Cloud) to create the dashboards you need, in our repository you can at least find a CDS-View you can use for extraction (ZKNSF_I_SCORING), that combines the findings with the score. If you want to directly extract e.g. a list aggregated by object, you could directly use another CDS-View (ZKNSF_I_DEVELOPMENT_OBJECTS).

 

What's next?

Kernseife is currently in use (or in prior versions) in several projects. But to be very clear: There is development ongoing, but we do not have a product roadmap available.

But as it is open source, we are open to feature discussions or pull requests - or just use it for your own advantage!

entry.title: SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2505
entry.content:
SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2505

With the introduction of the new release 2505, this blog post presents a comprehensive overview of the latest features and enhancements. The release has been available since May 17th and serves as the successor to the 2502 release. Some highlights of the 2505 release include the reduction of the technical downtime for release upgrades, the quick Fiori app generation within ABAP Development Tools (ADT) and the AI-based RAP BO Generator. 

The list of all new features can be found on the What's New for SAP BTP ABAP environment page. 

All new AI features are summarized here.

 

ABAP Development Tools (ADT)

  • Working with the log when migrating CDS DDIC-based views to CDS view entities, after finishing the simulation or migration of CDS DDIC-based views to CDS view entities, you can now filter the log on the result page using the Show Objects drop-down list box 
  • Restructuring the "Code Completion" and "Source Code Editors" preference pages 
  • RAP Business Objects Test Double Framework: Supported EML Statements
  • Data preview now supports ‘between’ capabilities in the filter options
  • Testing web API-based services using Swagger UI
  • Creating SAP Fiori App using Quick Fiori Application Generator
  • Improvements in the ABAP coverage view, new features: Changing the coverage scope and Exclude from coverage 
  • ADT Links can now be incorporated in the HTML result of an IDE action, allowing users to navigate to development objects in ADT 
  • ABAP SQL Consumption Model, you can it to generate corresponding CDS external entities for each entity that is exposed in a SQL service 
  • Creating and editing CDS table entities 

Core Data Services (CDS)

  • Improved CDS Buffering, optimize the ABAP memory consumption for scenarios which make use of CDS Entity Buffering 

OData

  • OData UI Service from Scratch, you can create all RAP-service related repository objects from scratch by using the OData UI Service from Scratch wizard 
  • Null value handling, you can now find an overview of the differences in the behavior of null values and initial values in OData V2 and V4 with regard to sorting and filtering on the Fiori Elements UI as well as explicit filtering through OData Web API request 

Data Archiving

  • Storage Manager Implementation, you can now use a new interface method to rollback faulty verified archive files (details)

Application Jobs

  • New Skip Function, you can now skip a single execution of a periodic job chain in the Application Job Templates app (details)

Units of measurement

  • You can now retrieve the standard units of measurement and dimensions delivered by SAP using released ABAP Cloud APIs (CL_UOM*) (details)

Business Configuration App

  • New Email Configuration for the Upload Business Configuration App (details)
  • Intent Navigation, if all technical key fields are provided as parameters to uniquely identify a record of an object page or subobject page, it's opened directly (details)

XCO Library

  • XCO XSLX: You can now style the fonts of a cell of an Excel worksheet (details)

Print Queue Administrator Integration

  • You can now create and delete print queues directly using APIs without the need to enter the Maintain Print Queues app, you can do so using the communication scenario SAP_COM_0A86 (details)

IAM Information System

  • You can now display the relevant components for business catalogs and applications. This way you can check under which component you can create an incident if required (details)

Released APIs and software component relations

  • You can now read about the best practices of using released APIs and software component relations on the SAP Help Portal (details)

Provider Support Role

  • Include start authorizations for custom services in provider support role 

Development Test Role

  • The business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_EXT_TST for development test roles is now continuously extended, not only with the start authorizations for custom services, but also with full authorizations for any custom authorization object included in the authorization default values 

Technical Monitoring

  • Capture and download of PLV files in ABAP cross trace for SQL statement execution analysis (details)

Message Monitoring

  • Message Monitoring for Integration Experts, you can now download the original payload of your message (details)

Custom Code Analysis

  • 3-Tier Model ATC Checks, there are now two new ATC checks of category 3-Tier Model: "Usage of APIs" and "Allowed Enhancement Technologies" (details)

Landscape Portal

  • Monitoring customers and products, the new Customer Overview app in the Landscape Portal enables partners to keep track of their customers and products (details)

SAP Fiori Launchpad

  • Extend the Sort Priority Field in the Manage Launchpad Spaces App, you can now extend the sort priority field to +/- 9999.99999 (details)
  • Avatars for Themes in the User Settings (details)
  • Hide Empty Spaces and Pages in the SAP Fiori Launchpad (details)

SAPUI5

  • Referenced SAPUI5 Version, as part of hotfix collection 3,  SAPUI5 version 1.133.2 is used for the administrator’s SAP Fiori launchpad, SAP-delivered Fiori apps, and the custom-built SAP Fiori apps, which are deployed to the ABAP system (see upgrade windows and SAPUI5)

SAP HANA Cloud

  • Increased SAP HANA Cloud Backup Retention Time to 30 Days (details)

entry.title: ✨ How to Identify SAP Fiori Apps Based on RAP (RESTful Application Programming Model)
entry.content:
📚Introduction

With the rise of SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP, the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) has become the gold standard for building cloud-ready, upgrade-safe applications. RAP brings together modern CDS views, OData V4 services, business object behavior definitions, and service bindings to simplify and accelerate application development.

As SAP increasingly delivers new Fiori apps using RAP—and updates existing ones into RAP-based versions—it's becoming critical for developers, architects, and even functional consultants to:

  • Identify which apps use RAP
  • Understand how to extend them using side-by-side or in-app extensibility
  • Recognize RAP-based capabilities like draft handling, validations, actions, and more

But here's the challenge: ❌SAP doesn't explicitly label Fiori apps as "RAP-based" in the Fiori Apps Library. So how can we reliably identify which apps are built using RAP?

This blog shows you how. Let's break it down with examples, tools, screenshots, and common pitfalls.

🔍Where to Begin? Fiori Apps Reference Library

Head to the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library. This is your primary tool for:

  • Searching apps by Line of Business (e.g., Finance, Procurement)
  • Filtering by SAP S/4HANA Version, UI Technology, and more
  • Getting details like OData service, App ID, and Extensibility options

⚠️But here's the catch: The library doesn't let you filter directly for "RAP-based apps". So you have to be smart about indirect clues.

 

 

 

 

 

⚡RAP Indicators in the Library

Apply these filters and clues:

  • UI Technology: Select SAP Fiori elements (RAP supports this by default)
  • Application Type: Select Transactional
  • OData Version: If available, filter or check for OData V4 (RAP requires this)
  • App Name or Version: Look for apps with names like:
    • "Manage Sales Quotation - Version 2"
    • "Manage Sales Contract - Version 2"

🔮Confirming RAP in the Backend (Step-by-Step)

If you've found a promising app in the Fiori Library, verify it in the backend using ADT (ABAP Development Tools) or SAP GUI.

✅Checklist: Is It RAP?

CheckpointRAP IndicatorTool

Service Binding exists

✅Required

ADT (Eclipse/BAS)

Service Definition used

✅Required

ADT

Behavior Definition present

✅Required

ADT

No SEGW project involved

✅Good sign

ADT or GUI

OData Version = 4.0

✅Strong hint

/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE

✅RAP-Based App Example

App ID F5630 (Manage Sales Quotation - Version 2):

  • OData = V4 ✅
  • Has Service Definition: UI_SALESQUOTATIONMANAGE✅
  • Has Service Binding: UI_SALESQUOTATIONMANAGE ✅
  • Has Behavior Definition: I_SalesQuotationTP ✅

🎉Conclusion: This is a RAP-based Fiori app.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

📊Diagram: How RAP-Based Fiori App Is Structured

UI_SALESQUOTATIONMANAGE (Service Binding, OData V4)

└── UI_SALESQUOTATIONMANAGE (Service Definition)

    └── [Projection CDS View – C_SalesQuotationManageTP]

        └── I_SalesQuotationTP (Root CDS View with Behavior)

            └── Behavior Definition (BDD)

✨Conclusion

Identifying whether a Fiori app is RAP-based takes a bit of detective work, but once you understand the key indicators—OData V4, service binding, behavior definition—you'll be able to:

  • Build smarter extensions
  • Create upgrade-stable extensions
  • Use modern features like drafts, validations, and late numbering

🚀Whether you're a developer or an architect planning your SAP S/4HANA roadmap, knowing how to filter and confirm RAP apps helps you take full advantage of the modern ABAP programming model.

If you found this blog helpful, share it with your team and Happy Learning Together !! 😉

entry.title: CDS: How to use @AnalyticsDetails.elimination annotation to eliminate the internal business volume
entry.content:
This blog is about how to use @AnalyticsDetails.elimination to eliminate the internal business volume when executing an analytical query.

Internal business volume

When revenues made between two subsidiaries of company, for company’s total revenue, these revenues should not be considered, as transaction is happening within the company. For example, a company is divided into several subsidiaries. These subsidiaries are divided into further subsidiaries. This means there exists a hierarchy of subsidiaries. Subsidiaries are selling products to each other. For each subsidiary this is external sales. But for a parent subsidiary this is just internal sales, as both subsidiaries are descendants. By using this annotation, revenues made between two subsidiaries in an organization are no longer displayed.

More information about the concept can be found Elimination of Internal Business Volume | SAP Help Portal

Syntax

elimination: {

     pair : array of {

       dimension1: String(30);

       dimension2: String(30);

     }

     rule : String(20) enum { ELIMINATE_IF_ALL; ELIMINATE_IF_ONE; };

   }

The annotation can be used in cube-views (Analytics.dataCategory: #CUBE) or in analytical queries (define transient view entity with provider contract analytical query).

  • pair: this list defines the pairs (dimension1 and dimension2) which are checked for elimination. Both dimensions have needed a foreign key association to the same target view. If the annotation is used on cube level, both dimensions must be elements of the cube. If the annotation is used in an analytical query, both dimensions must be part of the source cube view.
  • rule: if set to #ELIMINATE_IF_ALL, then the volume is eliminated if all pairs are internal. If set to #ELIMINATE_IF_ONE the volume is eliminated if at least one pair is internal. If "rule" is not set, the AND-logic (ELIMINATE_IF_ALL) is applied.

Example 

Following example displays the number of seat booked in flight according to the elimination rule specified on destination airport, departure airport, destination country, departure country.

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_ALLOWED
@EndUserText.label: 'Elimination of Internal Busniess volumn'
@ObjectModel.modelingPattern: #ANALYTICAL_QUERY
@ObjectModel.supportedCapabilities: [#ANALYTICAL_QUERY]
define transient view entity ZLR_PC_FLIGHTICE_SIMPLE
  provider contract analytical_query
  as projection on ZOQ_FLIGHT_ICE
{

  _airportfrom._hier( p_HierarchyID : 'GEO' ) as _airportFromHier,
  @Consumption.hidden: true
  _airportfrom.AirportID                      as dummyAirportFrom,


  _airportto._hier( p_HierarchyID : 'GEO' )   as _airportToHier,
  @Consumption.hidden: true
  _airportto.AirportID                        as dummyAirportTo,
  
  @AnalyticsDetails.query: {
    axis: #ROWS,
    displayHierarchy: #ON,
    hierarchyAssociation: '_airportFromHier'
  }
  
  airportfrom,
  
  @AnalyticsDetails.query: {
    axis: #ROWS,
    displayHierarchy: #ON,
    hierarchyAssociation: '_airportToHier'
  }
  airportto,

  @Consumption.filter.defaultValue: 'DE'
  @AnalyticsDetails.query.axis: #ROWS
  countryfrom,

  @AnalyticsDetails.query.axis: #ROWS
  countryto,

  seatsmax,



  @AnalyticsDetails.elimination: {
    pair: [{ dimension1 : 'countryfrom' , dimension2: 'countryto' }]
  }
  @EndUserText.label: 'SeatsMax Q-ICE country'
  seatsmax                                    as seatsmaxICEQ_C,


  @AnalyticsDetails.elimination:{
    pair: [ { dimension1 : 'airportfrom' , dimension2: 'airportto' },
            { dimension1 : 'countryfrom' , dimension2: 'countryto' } ],
    rule: #ELIMINATE_IF_ALL
  }
  @EndUserText.label: 'SeatsMax Q-ICE airport and country'
  seatsmax                                    as seatsmaxICEQ_AC,


  @AnalyticsDetails.elimination:{
    pair: [ { dimension1 : 'airportfrom' , dimension2: 'airportto' },
            { dimension1 : 'countryfrom' , dimension2: 'countryto' } ],
    rule: #ELIMINATE_IF_ONE
  }
  @EndUserText.label: 'SeatsMax Q-ICE airport or country'
  seatsmax                                    as seatsmaxICEQ_A_C


}

 

Output

 

In this example for columns (cells marked with colour): 

SeatsMax Q-ICE country – elimination pair mentioned as 'countryfrom' and 'countryto'. Therefore, there is no entry where departure country and destination country are same.

SeatsMax Q-ICE airport and country- elimination pairs are 'airportfrom' and 'airportto', 'countryfrom' and 'countryto' And rule as #ELIMINATE_IF_ALL, Therefore, there is no entry where destination airport and departure airport are same AND destination country and departure country are same.

SeatsMax Q-ICE airport or country - elimination pairs are 'airportfrom' and 'airportto', 'countryfrom' and 'countryto' And rule as #ELIMINATE_IF_ONE, Therefore, there is no entry where destination airport and departure airport are same OR destination country and departure country are same.

 

Constraints

The annotation can be used in cubes or in queries.

  • The dimension names in one pair (AnalyticsDetails.elimination.pair) must have a foreign key association to the same dimension view and this dimension view must have an hierarchy association.
  • In the query definition:
    • It is not necessary that any of the dimensions in the list of pairs is part of the query
    • If a hierarchy is assigned to a dimension, then the pair dimension must be assigned to the same hierarchy or must be without hierarchy
    • If a dimension is not in drill down, then the condition of the one pair is always true (independent of the value of the other dimension of the pair). The same is true on totals/subtotal lines.

entry.title: Building and Publishing External APIs in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public: From ADT to Postman
entry.content:
Overview

This guide explains how to create and expose a custom external API using the Developer Extensibility approach in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition. The process involves creating a custom business object, implementing its behavior, and publishing it as a service accessible to external systems.

 

Prerequisites

  • Access to SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition with developer extensibility enabled.
  • ABAP Development Tools (ADT) installed in Eclipse.
  • Business role SAP_BR_DEVELOPER assigned.
  • A development package created in the ABAP environment.

Step-by-Step Guide

Create a Custom Database Table: This is the foundational data structure where your application’s data will be stored. It represents the physical storage in the system.

  • In ADT, create a new database table (e.g., ZTODO_LIST).
  • Define required fields and activate the table.

Create a Root CDS View (Interface View): The root CDS view defines the business object’s main entity (the root node) and provides an abstraction over the database table. It enables the use of annotations and is required to define the behavior and expose data services.

  • Right-click your package > New > Data Definition.
  • Name it (e.g., Z_I_TODO_LIST).
  • Use defines root view syntax to expose your database table.
  • Annotate with @ObjectModel: and activate the view.

Create Behavior Definition with Managed Implementation: The behavior definition governs how the data can be interacted with (Create, Read, Update, Delete - CRUD). Using managed implementation reduces manual coding and allows SAP to handle the data persistence layer automatically.

  • Right-click on the CDS view > New Behavior Definition.
  • Mapping the CDS Fields with Custom table.

Create a Projection View: The projection view exposes only the fields and structure you want available to the external world via the API. It acts as the public API interface layered over the root CDS view and enables additional annotations like UI or access control.

  • Right-click > New > Data Definition.
  • Use define root view entity as projection

Create behavior definition for your Projection View: Determines what operations (Create, Update, Delete, Action, etc.) are exposed to consumers of the API.

Define a Service Definition: The service definition tells SAP which projection view (and hence, which data and operations) to expose as a service. This is a required layer before service binding can be created.

  • Right-click > New > Service Definition.

Create a Service Binding: The binding connects the service definition to a specific protocol (e.g., OData V4) and exposes the service endpoint URL.
It also allows you to publish the service so it's available in the system catalog.

  • Right-click > New > Service Binding.
  • Choose OData V4 as the binding type.
  • Name and activate the binding.
  • Click Publish to make it available.

Create a Communication Scenario: The communication scenario defines the APIs and authorizations needed for external communication. It acts as a container that external systems will use to discover and authenticate with your custom service.

  • In ADT, create a communication scenario.
  • Add your service binding.
  • Define inbound service, I/O structure, and authorization.
  • Activate and publish it.

Configure Communication Arrangement in SAP Fiori:  The communication arrangement ties the communication scenario to an actual communication system and user credentials.

Prerequisites:

  1. Create a New Communication User:
    1. Go to the Fiori Launchpad.
    2. Search for or open "Maintain Communication Users" app.
    3. Click on "New".
    4. Fill in the required fields
    5. Save the user.
  2. Create a Communication System:
    1. Go to your SAP Fiori Launchpad.
    2. Search for and open "Communication Systems".
    3. Users for Inbound Communication
    4. Click "New".
    5. Fill Communication Users (technical credentials).
    6. Save.
  • Open “Communication Arrangements” app in SAP Fiori Launchpad.
  • Create a new arrangement using your communication scenario.
  • Set communication system, user, and authentication method.
  • Save to generate the external API URL.

 

Test - Postman:

Conclusion

Building an external API in SAP S/4HANA Cloud (Public Edition) using Developer Extensibility gives developers the tools to connect seamlessly with external systems — securely, efficiently, and in line with SAP best practices. By following a structured, standards-compliant process, you create scalable, flexible APIs that support modern integration needs, third-party tools, and future innovations in the cloud landscape.

And hey — if your API doesn’t work the first time... don’t panic. Just turn it off, activate it again, and pretend it was a test all along. 😅

entry.title: How to add task to an existing transport request using program
entry.content:
PARAMETERS: p_TR TYPE trkorr OBLIGATORY. // Take TR as parameter

 

DATA: lt_authorlist TYPE trwbo_users,

lt_return TYPE e070-trkorr,

ls_return TYPE bapiret2,

lv_new_task TYPE trkorr. // Variable to take task

 

START-OF-SELECTION.

 

 

CALL FUNCTION 'TRINT_INSERT_NEW_COMM'

EXPORTING

wi_kurztext = 'New Task' // Description if creating TR

wi_trfunction = 'S' // 'S' to create task under TR

iv_username = sy-uname // System Username

wi_strkorr = p_TR // Export TR value to FM

IMPORTING

we_trkorr = lv_new_task // Import task to the variable

EXCEPTIONS

no_systemname = 1

no_systemtype = 2

no_authorization = 3

db_access_error = 4

file_access_error = 5

enqueue_error = 6

number_range_full = 7

invalid_input = 8

OTHERS = 9.

IF sy-subrc = 0.

WRITE: / ' New task created under TR:', p_TR,

/ ' Task number:', lv_new_task.

ELSE.

WRITE: / 'Failed to create task. '.

ENDIF.

*********************************************************************************
Type of request/task
Domain : TRFUNCTION
K : Workbench Request
W : Customizing Request
S : Development/Correction
Q : Customizing Task

entry.title: SAP CodeJam Roadshow 2025 - Latin America edition 🇧🇷 🇵🇪 🇲🇽 🇨🇴 (July 25th - Aug 12th 2025)
entry.content:
¡Hola SAP Developers!

I've got some exciting news to share with you. The SAP CodeJam roadshow is coming back to Latin America! This time, Josh Bentley, Rich Heilman, Thomas Jung, and Antonio Maradiaga will be part of the second SAP CodeJam Roadshow in Latin America.  We will have four stops on the roadshow starting the 25th of July and then continuing from August 4th - 12th, 2025, doing a total of seven CodeJams, one in São Paulo and then two in Lima, Mexico City and Bogota. Also, we'll organise a couple of quedadas (meetups) as we did last year. :-).

Check out the featured guests list in each event to learn which Developer Advocate(s) will be at the different events/locations.

 

SAP CodeJam ABAP Cloud

This SAP CodeJam event is on the topic of the ABAP Cloud, and specifically introduces attendees to ABAP Cloud and the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model. ABAP Cloud is the ABAP based development model for S/4HANA Cloud, S/4HANA on premise, and SAP BTP, ABAP environment. Learn how to use proven and new technologies, such as Core Data Services (CDS) and behavior definition and implementation languages, to build SAP Fiori apps and OData services on any of the ABAP Cloud options. In this hands-on experience, developers will create their first ABAP applications using ABAP Cloud, expose and consume services via HTTP, and explore the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model.

Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core

In this CodeJam, you will learn how to use Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core to implement a retrieval augmented generation (RAG) use case to improve the responses of large language models (LLMs) and reduce hallucinations. You will learn how to deploy an LLM and the orchestration service on SAP AI Core and query it via SAP AI Launchpad and the Python SDK. Furthermore, you will learn about the most important genAI concepts and create and use text chunks and embeddings to improve your RAG response.

SAP CodeJam events and quedadas (meetups) part of the roadshow (Register Now!)

Below is a listing of the SAP CodeJam events and quedadas (meetups) on the roadshow schedule:
